ah! this is not very elegant… What value does adding checking the type of the URL here? I can see a few ways to get rid of that issue, either:

* make the `request_create` method return a string `"\n{}".format('\n'.join(…))`, or
* make the `request_create` method always return a list, and modify the other services to match that, or
* make the `do_request_create` method delegate the way it displays stuff the same way we do with `list` methods (make that method a generator, where you first yield a format, then a header, then the values).

(I sorted the solution by order of laziness, the first one being the less work ☺)

Though, I believe the third option would be the most elegant, so that way you could control the exact message you want to output to the user to make git-repo more adapted to the gerrit way of things!

On further thought, I think that `datetime` does it right in the separation of naive and tz-aware times, and never the two shall meet (more or less).

Delorean should probably be a purist and only stick with tz-aware times.  For my use case (where I want to be aware when a naive time is parsed) I can use `raise` mode, and deal with it accordingly. Perhaps by using `dateutil.parser.parse()` directly.

If I'd rather use delorean exclusively for all my time needs, perhaps a parse_to_datetime function could be added which consistently returns a `datetime`, vs `parse()` which consistently returns a Delorean.

On the issue of garbage collection--it's a shame the gc module doesn't expose an API to check for a given object if it's reachable from outside the GC, without actually performing a full collection.

If nothing else the import statement should be moved outside this function--there's no reason for it to be inside.  We could also avoid an unnecessary full collection by checking `gc.get_referrers(cysigs.exc_value)`.  That doesn't necessarily guarantee that it's part of a reference cycle or that `collect()` would do anything to affect its reference count (e.g. it could be part of a cycle that includes an object with `__del__`).   But if it has no referrers tracked by the GC then obviously it's unreachable.

This change has the technical benefit of working in both cases without needing specific logic based on the branch name.

In terms of logic, it makes the assumption that all tagged commits are published. The previous implementation made the assumption that all commits on `master` are published. I am more confident that no accidental tagging will happen than no commits on `master` can accidentally happen.
In the same way, if we want to unpublish a version (or mark a version as unpublished), we now simply have to remove a tag. Removing a commit on `master` is harder :wink:

If you want added rigidity (which would go beyond what was already the case), I'm happy to add a check for the tag to be `x.y.z`, preventing preversions from being taken into account 🙂

My understanding of why nothing but a type-ascription on the function-call _inputs_ would work, is that that is the only position in which parameters can be generic/abstract (in the absence of `impl Trait` or boxing). With `impl Trait` for local variables, you _maybe_ could have type ascribed this as:
```
let stream: impl Stream<Item=_, Error=grpcio::Error> = ...
```

I've head `impl Trait` referred to as allowing for an "abstract return type", which is not quite the same as having a "generic return type". The caller of a method chooses a generic parameter, but the implementer of a method chooses an abstract return type.

The typical types of the HTTP/ProtocolException are:
```
[<class 'str'>, <class 'ConnectionResetError'>]
```

Typically the tuple is something like `('Connection aborted.', ConnectionResetError(104, 'Connection reset by peer'))`

You're right that the `type` of `ConnectionResetError` is that of `<class 'type'>`, but thats because you're getting the type of the *class*, not the object. The exception arg's tuple has an instance of the *object*, which has type `<class 'ConnectionResetError'>`

Yeah, the info for this is spread around Sections 3.22 and 4.20. This is how the state transition should go.

If the reason code is `KEY_COMPROMISE`:
`PRE_ACTIVE` -> `COMPROMISED`
`ACTIVE` -> `COMPROMISED`
`DEACTIVATED` -> `COMPROMISED`
`DESTROYED` -> `DESTROYED_COMPROMISED`

If the reason code is *not* `KEY_COMPROMISE`:
`ACTIVE` -> `DEACTIVATED`

If someone tries to revoke an object that is not in the `ACTIVE` state, for a reason that is *not* a compromise, that should be an error (`IllegalOperation` seems fine).

We've stated in the documentation how we define it:
```
'same' pads with half the filter size on both sides (one less on the second side for an even filter size). When stride=1, this results in an output size equal to the input size.
```

`_index_cache` is the descriptor and `_index_cache_dict` is the actual Python dict underlying it. Similarly for the `_data`.

The usual pattern here is for publicly accessible object, you would have an attribute called `foo` with some underlying data, perhaps stored in a class variable called `_foo` which would be accessed by:
```python
@property
def foo(self):
    return self._foo
```
Since `_data` and `_index_cache` are already semi-internal, a different name was needed.

In the old test system multipass tests were reserved for those tests which really *needed* to be multipass. All other tests (the majority) were .gpi files which were run independently. We still have some tests which need to by 'multipass' and for those the best solution is to name them `test_a_...`, `test_b_...` but they are in the minority.

It is prevalent in the Python testing world (and in all testing worlds really) that test functions should be runnable independently and in any order. This is why, for example, there's a `--last-failed` flag for pytest which only runs the tests that failed 'last time'. This breaks if we have dependent or ordered tests.

These tests here in this PR do not need to be multipass. The contents of `test_a_testClientSideSubmit` would be better described as a fixture (which could perhaps be parametrised and shared across test functions), `test_b_testClientSideComplete` would be a single test function, parametrised on the `LocalFileConfig['backendPostprocess']['Local']` setting and `cleanUp` would be a finaliser for the fixture. It would simplify the test code, make it more composable and extensible and allow the advertised test functions to be run independently in any order.

`TRNGPoll` could return an error from `TRNGStatusGet` on line 102. Instead we just spin in a busy while loop until the entropy pool is full enough, if it is ever full enough.

So `ethpm.package.fetch_ipfs_package` is a reference to `ethpm.utils.ipfs.fetch_ipfs_package`.  When you monkeypatch the later, everything that *already* had a reference to it retains that reference.  It's basically a race.  *if* you were able to do the monkeypatch *before* loading the `epthm.package` module, then `ethpm.package.fetch_ipfs_package` would end up being the monkeypatched version (and would remain the monkeypatched version *after* the monkeypatch had been removed.

You can get around this by not importing it directly, but rather importing the module.

```python
# ethpm/package.py
from ethpm.utils import ipfs

def thing(...):
    ipfs.fetch_ipfs_package(...)
```

In this scenario, the `package` module resolves `ipfs.fetch_ipfs_package` everytime it uses it, and thus, if you monkeypatch it, then the `package` module will use the monkeypatched version.

Basically, python is mutable as hell and it gets really confusing when we start mutating modules like this.
